sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content: Surgical resection is an option for a select subset of patients with very early-stage SCLC (clinical stage I–IIA, T1–2,N0,M0) after comprehensive staging. It is not a standard treatment for most SCLC patients due to the aggressive nature and early metastatic potential of the disease [1].

cards.name: Lobectomy with mediastinal lymph node dissection or systematic lymph node sampling
cards.condition: Clinical stage I–IIA (T1–2,N0,M0) SCLC after negative pathologic mediastinal staging; considered for T3 (size) N0 if invasive mediastinal staging is negative
cards.contraindications: Not standard for most SCLC patients; node-positive disease (N1/N2); advanced stage beyond I–IIA; pneumonectomy if needed to encompass nodal metastatic disease
cards.detail: Approach: Open or minimally invasive (thoracoscopic). Systematic sampling should include ≥3 N2 and ≥1 N1 stations [1]. Key complications: Standard surgical risks: bleeding, infection, pneumothorax, prolonged air leak, bronchopleural fistula, arrhythmia, and mortality [1]. Operative mortality: Not specified in source; lobectomy mortality is generally low (1–3%) in high-volume centers. Intraoperative diagnosis of likely SCLC: perform mediastinal lymph node dissection or sampling with frozen section; if resectable, perform anatomic resection, preferably lobectomy. Pneumonectomy should not be performed if needed to encompass nodal metastatic disease [1]. Patients who undergo complete resection should be treated with postoperative systemic therapy [1]. In patients who do not smoke, small lesions presumed to be SCLC on biopsy should be resected because they are likely carcinoids that have been misdiagnosed [1].
